FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (Data Release 5.1, 21 recruitment sites, baseline ages 9-10); methods state: "This retrospective cohort study uses data from the ABCD study, which is an ongoing and 10-year longitudinal study of neurocognitive development in adolescents... the ABCD study used school-based probability sampling to recruit its baseline cohort between 2016 and 2018... enrolled adolescents aged 9-10 years from 21 sites across the United States."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data using linear mixed models (LMMs) to examine longitudinal associations among cyberbullying victimization (T1), inhibitory control, fMRI ROI activation during error processing (T2), and mental health outcomes (T2); mediation analyses also conducted.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Medical Internet Research (2026); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No results table or supplementary table presents a numeric estimate (beta, OR, r, etc.) where the variable label is phone/SM-specific (time on SM, named platform, texting, video chat, app use, MPIQ, SMAQ, etc.) paired with an individual-trait variable. Social media use appears only as a covariate (Table 1 reports group means; methods state it was included as a covariate in all analyses). The primary exposure is cyberbullying victimization (yes/no), which is not a qualifying phone/SM variable per binding clarification 3: 'Cyberbullying alone does not qualify.' Step 2 not applicable (no composite screen-time variable). FT-IC2 = NOT_MET.

notes: Paper is about cyberbullying victimization as the primary exposure predicting fMRI neural activation and mental health outcomes. Social media is controlled for as a covariate, not analyzed as IV or DV with its own coefficient. Cyberbullying alone does not meet FT-IC2 per binding clarification.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (Data Release 4.0); methods state: "This study used data from the ABCD study (Data Release 4.0), an ongoing longitudinal study beginning in 2016 across 21 research sites in the US." Baseline mean age 9.5 years, N=8979.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using Gaussian Graphical Models (GGM) for cross-sectional networks and Graphical Vector Autoregressive Models (GVAR) for temporal networks on three waves of ABCD data (baseline, Year 1, Year 2).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology (2026); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: The temporal network (GVAR) reports separate quantitative edge weights for social media use predicting irritability over time (edge weight = 0.12) and for online chatting (texting + video chat combined) predicting irritability (edge weight = -0.03), as well as irritability predicting online chatting (edge weight = 0.03). Social media is a qualifying phone/SM variable (named SM platform use); irritability is a qualifying individual-trait variable (behavioral/mental health). These are bidirectional analyses where SM appears as both IV and DV in the temporal model.

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative network edge weights reported throughout: e.g., social media -> irritability = 0.12 (temporal); online chat -> irritability = -0.03; irritability -> online chat = 0.03; cross-sectional GGM edge weight for irritability-video streaming = -0.03 at Year 2. Bootstrapped confidence intervals and stability checks also reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses performed using GGM and GVAR network models with numeric edge weights, bootstrapped accuracy checks, and centrality indices; results reported for SM and individual-trait associations.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply for two independent reasons: (1) the paper contains cross-sectional GGM analyses at each of three timepoints where SM/screen activities appear as nodes (cross-sectional carve-out applies); (2) the temporal GVAR model is bidirectional, with SM as both IV (social media -> irritability, online chat -> irritability) and DV (irritability -> online chat), so SM is not DV-only.
notes: Screen time measured using ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey with six separate activities including texting, video chatting (combined as 'online chat'), and social media platforms (e.g., Instagram). Irritability is measured via CBCL items. SM-as-IV and SM-as-DV both present in bidirectional GVAR model; cross-sectional GGMs also present at each wave.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (Data Release 5.1); methods state: "Participants were from the ongoing ABCD study (https://abcdstudy.org/; baseline assessment of ABCD [release 5.1]). This extensive longitudinal study has recruited over 11,500 children aged 9-11 years across 21 sites in the United States." Final sample n=7043.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using residual change score models and linear mixed-effects models (lmer) to assess longitudinal associations between modifiable lifestyle factors (including screen time) and cognitive development from baseline to 2-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescence (2026); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Social media is analyzed as a separate predictor with its own coefficient in Tables 3 and 4. E.g., Table 3: social media -> inhibitory control and attention (B = -0.50, SE = 0.21, p = 0.017, pFDR = 0.026*); social media -> information processing speed (B = -0.99, SE = 0.41, p = 0.015, pFDR = 0.039*); social media -> visuospatial sequencing and memory (B = -1.40, SE = 0.30, p < 0.001, pFDR < 0.001*). Table 4: social media -> verbal intellect (B = -0.79, SE = 0.15, p < 0.001, pFDR < 0.001*); social media -> reading and language ability (B = -0.28, SE = 0.11, p = 0.014, pFDR = 0.019*). Social media is a qualifying SM variable; cognitive outcomes (inhibitory control, processing speed, verbal intellect, reading ability) are individual-trait variables.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression B coefficients with SE, t values, p values, and pFDR correction reported in Tables 3, 4, and 5 for associations between social media and cognitive outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential regression analyses performed with linear mixed-effects models; numeric results reported including coefficients, standard errors, t-values, and FDR-corrected p-values for social media and cognitive development associations.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply: SM (social media) is the IV predicting cognitive outcomes as DV throughout all analyses. SM is never the DV; cognitive scores are the DV. Condition (b) of FT-EC7 (SM as DV in every analysis) is not met.
notes: Screen time measured using ABCD Study Screen Time Questionnaire with four separate categories: passive watching (TV/movies), gaming (computer/console/phone), social engagement (texting + video chat), and social media (Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, etc.). Each is analyzed separately as a predictor. Social media has its own coefficient in all main tables. Cognitive outcomes include NIH Toolbox tasks (FCF and CCF domains).
